text: This operation depicts a very careful dissection of the thymus gland in the chest. Very interesting, this girl actually presented with difficulty in walking down the stairs. So why would I dig into her chest if she couldn't walk down the stairs? Well friends, she couldn't walk down the stairs because of a condition known as myasthenia gravis. Myasthenia gravis means a very severe and grave weakness of the muscles. Why were her muscles weak? Because inside this thymus there was a tumor that was releasing some special antibodies. What were those antibodies doing? They were attacking her junctions of nerves and muscles, the so-called neuromuscular junction, and making her very, very weak. Therefore, the thymoma or tumor in the thymus had to be removed along with the rest of the thymus.

The surgeon performs a thymectomy using three small incisions in the chest, employing ultrasonic shears to gently dissect and remove the thymus and tumor without burning the tissue. The heart is visible, pumping beneath the surgical field, highlighting the need for careful maneuvering around the tumor.

But on the contrary, this poor girl kept slipping back onto the ventilator, which means she was not able to breathe for herself. So what did we do? A little bit of research showed us that she could be having long-acting antibodies released much earlier by the thymus. Knowing this, we decided to exchange and take out all plasma in her blood because the plasma was probably carrying these antibodies when we completely took out the plasma and did what is called a plasma pharesis within twenty four hours this young girl was back to normal and we could finally take out the tube once and for all

namely the tube that was allowing her to breathe through the ventilator we could take that off free her from the ventilator dependence that she was earlier going to and sort her problem out completely so this is the thoracoscopic thymectomy or removal of the thymus gland in total being done for a young girl with a tumor in the chest. Thymomas present in many ways, but the commonest way in which they present is with a tumor in the chest and also with a difficulty in walking, difficulty in breathing, especially as

The surgeon carefully dissects the thymus gland, revealing a large vein adjacent to the glandular structure. The small veins are meticulously managed to prevent excessive bleeding during the thoracoscopic thymectomy procedure.
